title: Level Assessment
description: Used to determine starting level for new users

questions.question: Which is spelled correctly?
questions.explanation: After C, write EI (not IE): receive.

questions.question: 'She is _____ a letter.'
questions.explanation: Drop the silent E before -ing: write → writing.

questions.question: Which is correctly punctuated?
options: I love reading, and she loves writing.
I love reading and, she loves writing.
I love reading and she loves writing,
I love reading. And she loves writing.
questions.explanation: Two complete sentences joined by FANBOYS 'and' need a comma before the conjunction.

questions.question: '___ going to ___ house.'
questions.explanation: They're = they are. Their = belonging to them.

questions.question: 'The group of students ___ very excited.'
questions.explanation: 'group' is a singular collective noun → is

questions.question: Which is a complete sentence?
options: She ran to the bus stop.
questions.explanation: C has subject (She) + verb (ran) + complete thought.

questions.question: Identify the error: 'I was tired, I went to bed.'
questions.explanation: A comma splice is two sentences incorrectly joined by only a comma. Needs FANBOYS: 'I was tired, so I went to bed.'

questions.question: Which is appropriate for a formal email?
options: I won't be able to attend tomorrow.
I am writing to inform you that I will be unable to attend tomorrow.
Tomorrow doesn't work for me, sorry!
questions.explanation: C uses no contractions, formal language, and states the purpose clearly.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'Dear Mr. Johnson, I'm writing to inquire about the position.'
questions.explanation: In formal writing, contractions should be avoided. Use 'I am writing' instead of 'I'm writing'.
